title: Electrolysis — How electricity splits compounds apart
examTip: AQA Chemistry Paper 1, Topic 4 (Chemical Changes). Expect questions on products at each electrode for molten and aqueous solutions, half equations, and industrial applications — especially aluminium and brine.
introTips: What electrolysis is and when ionic compounds can be electrolysed
How to predict what forms at each electrode for molten and aqueous solutions
Why electrolysis matters industrially — aluminium, copper, and chlorine
intro: Some metals and compounds are so stable that heat and carbon can't extract or decompose them — you need electricity instead. Electrolysis is the technique that lets us break apart ionic compounds using electrical energy, and it's why we can make aluminium (vital for planes, drink cans, and phones), purify copper for electrical cables, and produce the chlorine used in water treatment. Understanding which ions go where — and why — is one of the most predictable parts of the AQA exam once you know the rules.

keyPoints.heading: What electrolysis is — and why ions must be free to move
keyPoints.content: Electrolysis is the decomposition of an ionic compound using electrical energy. A direct current is passed through the compound, which causes it to break down. For electrolysis to work, the ions must be free to move — which only happens when the ionic compound is either molten (melted) or dissolved in water (aqueous solution). In a solid ionic compound, ions are locked in a fixed lattice and cannot move, so solid ionic compounds cannot be electrolysed. The mixture or liquid being electrolysed is called the electrolyte. Two electrodes (conductors) are dipped into the electrolyte and connected to a power supply — the cathode (negative electrode) and the anode (positive electrode).
writeBullets: Electrolysis — decomposition of an ionic compound using electrical energy
Electrolyte — the molten or aqueous ionic compound being broken down
Molten — ionic compound melted so ions are free to move
Aqueous solution — ionic compound dissolved in water so ions are free to move
Solid ionic compound — ions locked in lattice, cannot be electrolysed
Cathode — negative electrode; Anode — positive electrode

keyTerms.def: Breaking down an ionic compound using electrical energy
The molten or dissolved ionic compound that conducts electricity and is decomposed
Melted — ions are free to move even without water

keyPoints.memTip: Think of electrolysis as 'electricity does the splitting' — but only when ions can actually travel to the electrodes. Locked in a solid? No movement, no reaction. Cover your notes and write all 3 conditions from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The most common early mistake is saying 'ionic compounds always conduct electricity.' They only conduct — and can only be electrolysed — when molten or in solution. Solid ionic compounds do NOT conduct.

keyPoints.heading: Cathode and anode — which ions go where, and what happens
keyPoints.content: Opposite charges attract: cations (positive ions) are attracted to the cathode (negative electrode), and anions (negative ions) are attracted to the anode (positive electrode). At the cathode, cations gain electrons — this is reduction. At the anode, anions lose electrons — this is oxidation. The memory tool is OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons), Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). A second memory hook: Cathode = Cations (both start with C), and Anions go to the Anode (both start with A). Half equations show what happens at each electrode — for example, at the cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper ion gains 2 electrons to form copper metal).
writeBullets: Cation — positive ion, attracted to cathode (negative electrode)
Anion — negative ion, attracted to anode (positive electrode)
Reduction — gain of electrons, happens at the cathode
Oxidation — loss of electrons, happens at the anode
OIL RIG — Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain
Cathode = Cations (both C); Anions → Anode (both A)

keyTerms.def: Positively charged ion — attracted to the negative cathode
Negatively charged ion — attracted to the positive anode
Gain of electrons — occurs at the cathode
Loss of electrons — occurs at the anode
Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — the key memory tool for electron transfer

keyPoints.memTip: OIL RIG is your best friend here. Write it on your hand if you have to. Then: Cathode/Cation, Anode/Anion — same first letter. Cover your notes and write all 4 pairings from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often get cathode and anode mixed up. Anchor it: cathode is negative (think: c-ath-ode = c-old = negative vibes). The positive anode pushes positive ions away and pulls in negative ones.

keyPoints.heading: Electrolysis of molten ionic compounds
keyPoints.content: When a pure ionic compound is melted, only its own ions are present — so the products are straightforward to predict. Take lead bromide (PbBr₂): when molten, Pb²⁺ ions travel to the cathode and each gains 2 electrons to form lead metal (Pb). Br⁻ ions travel to the anode, each loses 1 electron, and pairs of bromine atoms form bromine gas (Br₂). The rule is simple: whatever metal cation is present is deposited as the metal at the cathode. Whatever anion is present is discharged at the anode as the non-metal element. Half equations: Cathode: Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb. Anode: 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻. Notice the anode half equation is written as loss of electrons (oxidation).
writeBullets: Molten lead bromide — only Pb²⁺ and Br⁻ ions present
Cathode product — lead metal (Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb)
Anode product — bromine gas (2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻)
Rule — metal cation always deposits as the metal at cathode
Rule — anion discharged as the element at anode
Simple molten electrolysis — no competition between ions

keyTerms.def: PbBr₂ — a common molten electrolysis example in AQA papers
An equation showing what happens at one electrode, including electrons gained or lost
When an ion gives up or receives electrons at an electrode, becoming an element

keyPoints.memTip: For molten compounds: just look at the formula. PbBr₂ → lead at cathode, bromine at anode. No tricks. Cover your notes and write the two half equations for lead bromide electrolysis from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Molten electrolysis is the simpler case — examiners love it as a starter question. Make sure you can write balanced half equations. The anode equation catches students out because you write it with electrons on the right (they're being lost, not gained).

keyPoints.heading: Electrolysis of aqueous solutions — competing ions
keyPoints.content: When an ionic compound is dissolved in water, water itself provides additional H⁺ and OH⁻ ions, so there are now competing ions at each electrode. You need rules to decide which ion 'wins'. At the cathode: H⁺ ions are normally discharged (producing hydrogen gas, H₂), UNLESS the metal ion present is less reactive than hydrogen (below hydrogen in the reactivity series) — in that case the metal is deposited instead. At the anode: OH⁻ ions are normally discharged (producing oxygen gas, O₂), UNLESS a halide ion (Cl⁻, Br⁻, or I⁻) is present in high concentration — in that case the halogen is produced instead. Key examples: Copper sulfate solution — copper (below hydrogen in reactivity) deposits at cathode; oxygen produced at anode (no halide). Brine (NaCl solution) — hydrogen produced at cathode (sodium too reactive to deposit); chlorine produced at anode (high concentration of Cl⁻ ions). The remaining solution becomes sodium hydroxide (NaOH).
writeBullets: Aqueous solutions — water adds H⁺ and OH⁻ ions creating competition
Cathode rule — H⁺ discharged (H₂ gas) unless metal is less reactive than hydrogen
Anode rule — OH⁻ discharged (O₂ gas) unless high concentration halide ion present
Copper sulfate — copper at cathode, oxygen at anode
Brine (NaCl) — hydrogen at cathode, chlorine at anode, NaOH solution remains
Sodium — too reactive, so H₂ produced at cathode not Na metal

keyTerms.def: A compound dissolved in water — water contributes H⁺ and OH⁻ ions
Concentrated sodium chloride (NaCl) solution — used in the chlor-alkali industry
The ranking of metals by how readily they react — determines cathode product in aqueous electrolysis

keyPoints.memTip: Two rules to memorise: Cathode — metal wins IF it's below hydrogen. Anode — halide wins IF it's in high concentration. Otherwise H₂ at cathode, O₂ at anode. Cover your notes and apply the rules to brine and copper sulfate from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is the hardest part of electrolysis for most students. The anode rule — 'high concentration halide beats OH⁻' — is easy to forget. In dilute NaCl, you actually get O₂ at the anode, not Cl₂. Concentration matters.

keyPoints.heading: Industrial uses of electrolysis
keyPoints.content: Electrolysis has three major industrial applications at AQA level. (1) Extraction of aluminium: aluminium is too reactive to reduce with carbon, so molten aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃) is electrolysed. It's dissolved in cryolite to lower the melting point from 2050°C to around 900°C — making it far less energy-intensive. Aluminium forms at the cathode; oxygen at the anode slowly burns away the carbon anode electrodes, which need regular replacement. Very energy-intensive — explains why aluminium recycling saves 95% of energy. (2) Purification of copper: impure copper is used as the anode; pure copper is the cathode; copper sulfate solution is the electrolyte. Copper dissolves from the impure anode and deposits as pure copper on the cathode. Impurities fall to the bottom as 'anode sludge' (which often contains valuable metals like silver and gold). (3) Chlor-alkali industry (electrolysis of brine): produces three useful products — chlorine gas (at anode — used for bleach, PVC, disinfecting water), hydrogen gas (at cathode — used as fuel, making margarine), and sodium hydroxide solution (remaining liquid — used for making soap, paper, and further chemical manufacture).
writeBullets: Aluminium extraction — electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ dissolved in cryolite
Cryolite — lowers melting point of Al₂O₃ from 2050°C to ~900°C
Carbon anodes — burn away in oxygen and must be replaced regularly
Copper purification — impure copper anode dissolves, pure copper deposits on cathode
Brine electrolysis — chlorine (anode), hydrogen (cathode), NaOH solution
Chlorine uses — bleach, PVC, water treatment; hydrogen — fuel, margarine; NaOH — soap, paper

keyTerms.def: A mineral used to dissolve aluminium oxide, lowering its melting point to make electrolysis feasible
Industrial electrolysis of brine producing chlorine, hydrogen, and sodium hydroxide
Impurities (including precious metals) that fall to the bottom during copper purification

keyPoints.memTip: Three industries: Al extraction (molten Al₂O₃ + cryolite), Cu purification (impure → pure), Chlor-alkali (brine → Cl₂ + H₂ + NaOH). For brine: think CHlorine-Hydrogen-NaOH = CHN. Cover your notes and name all three industries and their products from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Examiners frequently ask 'why is cryolite used?' — your answer must say 'lowers the melting point' not just 'helps it melt.' Also, be ready to explain why aluminium can't be extracted with carbon: it's too reactive, forming stable oxides.

0.question: Why can't solid lead bromide be electrolysed?
options: Its ions are locked in fixed positions and cannot move
It is not soluble in water
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. In a solid ionic compound, the ions are held in a rigid lattice and cannot move to the electrodes — no movement means no electrolysis.
0.wrongFeedback: Solid lead bromide definitely contains ions (Pb²⁺ and Br⁻), and it does have an ionic structure. The problem is that those ions cannot move in the solid state — they're locked in a fixed lattice. Electrolysis needs free-moving ions, which only happens when the compound is melted or dissolved.

3.question: During electrolysis of brine (concentrated NaCl solution), what is produced at the cathode?
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Sodium is more reactive than hydrogen, so H⁺ ions from water are preferentially discharged at the cathode, producing hydrogen gas.
3.wrongFeedback: At the cathode, the competition is between Na⁺ ions and H⁺ ions from water. Because sodium is more reactive than hydrogen (higher in the reactivity series), H⁺ ions win — they are preferentially discharged, producing hydrogen gas. Sodium metal is not deposited. Chlorine forms at the anode, and oxygen would form at the anode if there were no halide ions present.

4.question: Why is cryolite used in the industrial extraction of aluminium?
options: It acts as a source of aluminium ions
It prevents the carbon anodes from burning away
It lowers the melting point of aluminium oxide to around 900°C
It increases the concentration of aluminium at the cathode
4.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Aluminium oxide melts at over 2000°C, which is impractical and hugely expensive. Dissolving it in cryolite reduces the melting point to around 900°C, making the process commercially viable.
4.wrongFeedback: Cryolite is used because aluminium oxide has an extremely high melting point (over 2000°C). Melting it directly would require enormous energy and cost. Cryolite acts as a solvent, dissolving the aluminium oxide and lowering the melting point to around 900°C. The carbon anodes do burn away (in the oxygen produced at the anode) but cryolite doesn't prevent this.

commonMistakes: Saying solid ionic compounds conduct electricity — they don't; ions are immobile in a solid lattice
Confusing cathode and anode: cathode is NEGATIVE (attracts positive cations); anode is POSITIVE (attracts negative anions)
Forgetting the competing ions in aqueous solutions — you must apply the reactivity series rule at the cathode and the halide concentration rule at the anode
In brine electrolysis, saying sodium is produced at the cathode — sodium is too reactive; hydrogen gas is produced instead
Writing half equations with electrons on the wrong side — at the cathode electrons are gained (written on left: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu); at the anode electrons are lost (written on right: 2Br⁻ → Br₂ + 2e⁻)
examTips: Always state whether you're dealing with a molten or aqueous compound before predicting products — the rules are different for each
For half equations, check your electron balance: the number of electrons lost at the anode must equal the number gained at the cathode in the overall process
When asked about aluminium extraction, always mention cryolite and explain that it lowers the melting point — examiners want the reason, not just the name
revisionCardBullets: Electrolysis — decomposition of ionic compound using electrical energy
Ions must be free to move: molten OR aqueous solution (not solid)
Cathode = negative electrode; attracts cations (positive ions); reduction occurs (gain of electrons)
Anode = positive electrode; attracts anions (negative ions); oxidation occurs (loss of electrons)
OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain
Molten electrolysis: metal at cathode, non-metal element at anode (simple — no competition)
Aqueous cathode rule: H₂ gas forms UNLESS metal is less reactive than hydrogen (then metal deposits)
Aqueous anode rule: O₂ gas forms UNLESS high concentration halide present (then halogen forms)
Brine electrolysis products: Cl₂ (anode), H₂ (cathode), NaOH solution remains
Aluminium extraction: molten Al₂O₃ dissolved in cryolite (lowers m.p. from 2050°C to ~900°C)
Copper purification: impure anode → dissolves; pure cathode → copper deposits; impurities form anode sludge
Chlor-alkali uses: Cl₂ → bleach/PVC; H₂ → fuel/margarine; NaOH → soap/paper
